segment-text: JACK GOES BOATING
Cooper's Town Prods.
EXT. CLASSIC LIMO SERVICE PARKING LOT - DAY A cold winter day. Jack and Clyde in separate limos pointed

in different directions parked side by side near the exit of the lot. In background, Classic office. Clyde, Jack's friend and coworker, is well groomed, and wears a black limo suit -a little better kept suit than Jack's. Clyde passes a lit joint from out his window to his friend. A reggae song, Rivers of Babylon, is heard playing in Jack's limo on an old style tape portable player.
You thought about it?
(nervous throat clearing) A-hem...
(after a pause) ...I think you should.
What's she do there?
She's the assistant to the
Aw, no...
...something with the fluids. No, aw....
No, man. Lucy's training her to sell seminars for Dr. Bob.
Phone sales?
He does seminars for the funeral
I thought he was just an undertaker.
Jack passes the joint back through his car window to Clyde, who takes a final toke and discards the ends. She calls the funeral directors.
Cool. What's her name? Connie. on.
So, I'll tell Lucy it's
Jack sees a man, his uncle Frank, emerge from the office of Classic and head their way.
(indicates Uncle Frank) Here's my Uncle. Don't mention it -Hey.
That it's...y'know... because... I won't.
He won't let it rest.
Not a word.
It's how he is...
Uncle Frank appears outside of Jack's car window. the men a small wave and a knowing look. He gives
You two talking about me?
No.
Only about the money we make you.
That's allowed. Your lips to God's ears.
He turns and walks back toward the Classic office. Jack turns up the volume on Rivers of Babylon. Listens with serious appreciation. Jack detects a warble in the tape.
The tape's getting stretched.
You should go CD. Probably.
Go high tech. Yeah.
Clyde observes Jack mouthing the words.
Jack turns it down.
Would you call yourself a rasta man? No.
You thinking of becoming one? No.
I just wanted to ask.
You like the song? positive.
Reggae's mainly
Some of the words, you know, I don't get, so it's hard to commit.
"Over I" is a hard one. "Over I?"
"Over I," yeah. We'll just order something and hang out. Yeah. No biggie.
Clyde drives off. Jack listens to the song a moment, drives away.
INT/EXT. JACK'S LIMO - DAY

Jack drives. Italian woman in rear. Boxes and bags of purchases. The Italian woman is on her cell phone, talking in Italian. Her short talk is punctuated by the names of high end clothing stores, Barneys, etc. She hangs up, with kisses. After a moment-ITALIAN WOMAN You drive long time? Yeah.
You married?
I practice my English. Sorry. That's OK.
I love New York. People are nice. In Rome. Awful. Rude.
EXT. MANHATTAN STREET - DAY
Jack stands at the opened door as the Italian woman puts packages into the limo.
"Dressing for success." That's right?
Yes, ma'am. Dress for success.
Dress for success. Maybe shoes now.
She climbs into the limo.
EXT. WALDORF ASTORIA

A valet drives away the car of a newly arrived couple, who carry gift wrapped packages. Immediately following, another car pulls up, and behind it, Clyde's limo is in line, ready to deliver his passengers.
INT. CLYDE'S LIMO - NIGHT

Clyde eyes the happy couple in the back seat from the vantage of the rear view mirror. A young daughter of five between them. She has an extravagantly wrapped present on her lap, and is playing with the bow. Leave the pretty bow alone,
EXT. WALDORF ASTORIA - NIGHT
Clyde pulls the car up to the valet station. He gets out and opens the door for the happy family. They exit the car and enter the hotel.
the men's room? Pull it out of the way. And don't
He hustles into the limo to pull it out of the way.
INT. WALDORF ASTORIA MENS ROOM - NIGHT

An attendant stands by the opulent wash basins. Clyde finishes at a urinal and moves to wash his hands. The attendant offers Clyde a squeeze of a French hand soap from a fancy looking bottle. Clyde washes his hands. The attendant smiles and readies a towel. Clyde dries his hands. After another moment of the two men looking at one another, more or less expressionless, Clyde takes some change out of his pocket. No coins. The attendant steps back. Clyde puts a buck in a tip bowl and exits.
SECOND LOBBY, WALDORF ASTORIA - NIGHT
Clyde looks into and enters busy second lobby. He notices busboys/pastry assistants going into Empire Room. Clyde follows them.
SECOND LOBBY/EMPIRE ROOM - (CONTINUOUS)

Clyde looks in at the room catered for a wedding reception. Gifts. Guests, including the couple and child he drove who are at a table picking up id name tags, ("Hi, I'm Nonnie Plimpton".) Clyde enters the lobby. A small gathering of the well-dressed arrivals for the event sip cocktails and chat with each other near a pianist playing appropriate music. A trolley of desserts passes Clyde. Guests part to make way for it. A very tall pastry chef in white is revealed giving directions on the placement of the desserts. Clyde observes the man. After a moment, young woman with a guest list approaches Clyde. Her smile hides her obvious opinion that Clyde doesn't belong.
Clyde heads for the exit.
INT. DR. BOB'S MORTUARY BASEMENT AREA - DAY
Connie is on a phone at a work space studying with nervous intensity a form of instructions titled, "Overcoming Objections."
Hi. I'm calling from Dr. Bob's Grief Seminars. Dr. Bob's. Yes. Is this Mr. Pendecker? Did you get Dr. Bob's flyer on the upcoming seminar in Phoenix? Uh-huh. Uh-huh.
INT. ANOTHER ANGLE ON BASEMENT - CONTINUOUS

Lucy, around Connie's age, and her immediate boss, walks past two phone sale workers at their separate work stations that are making calls and giving pitches for Dr. Bob's seminar/workshop services to other funeral directors across the nation. She gives one a thumbs up as she takes a confirmation slip from her and moves on by -LUCY Someone's getting the bonus. Lucy walks to the open office door to Dr. Bob's office. Dr. Bob dips into view as he gives Lucy a quick congratulatory hug. At the same time, he looks over at Connie, struggling at the phone.
What's the verdict?
The jury's still out.
Lucy turns and crosses to Connie's work area.
Well, Dr. Bob's not here, right this minute, but I...
(quietly emphatic) Credit Card.
The new seminar experience offers techniques-- that's right-"violent acts... The pain of sudden deaths--" I did?
Credit Card.
Oh, ah-- Mr. Pendecker? (to Lucy) He said to call back tomorrow.
Remember, it's "Hello, the opening, gimme the credit card, the pitch, blah blah, gimme the credit card..." it's a mantra...
(repeats it quietly) Gimme the credit card.
They want you to give them permission to invest in themselves.
Dr. Bob appears, places a hand of reassurance on Connie's shoulder.
Another peek at the website might help with the calls. Get you familiar with what we do. (moving on) Night, night.
Dr. Bob heads out and down the hall to leave the mortuary.
INT. DR. BOB'S OFFICE, BASEMENT AREA -

Connie is watching the Dr. Bob website on a computer. The office is decorated with Dr. Bob's prominent memberships in various national councils related to his profession, degrees, certificates, etc. Family pictures on his desk.
INT. DR. BOB'S MORTUARY BASEMENT AREA
Lucy's just outside the office tending to some final paperwork.
Jack's a sweetheart. We've known him a long time. You like Chinese?
INT. DR. BOB'S OFFICE, BASEMENT AREA
Connie is not sure what she's heard.
He's Chinese? I think. I've not
We'll just order some.

Website headings: Journey Through Grief-- Life and Loss -appear and dissolve on the screen in a stylish manner, followed by site choices: "Estate Planning, Cremation Workshop, Grief Certification..." Connie hits one of the headings "A message from Dr. Bob," and an image of Dr. Bob comes on in a bucolic setting.
Hi, I'm Bob Thomas. My great grandfather started as a cabinet maker and undertaker in the small town of Pacerville, Oregon in 1885--
INT. DR. BOB'S MORTUARY BASEMENT AREA - DAY
Lucy is shutting down the area in preparation to leave. From inside Dr Bob's office the "A Message from Dr. Bob" is heard continuing.

For three generations Thomas Mortuaries have lovingly cared for the needs of family, and of loved ones, in short-- service -- a tradition for one hundred twentyfive years...offering a supporting hand under life's final transition. Bob Thomas Mortuary is a humble partner in helping you see to all your needs in affordable and complete funeral service.
Time to get out of here.
INT. DR. BOB'S OFFICE - DAY
Lucy enters.
Tonight will be low-key. I think you'll like Jack.
Connie signs off. Dr. Bob's screen saver pops on.
INT. JACK'S BASEMENT APT - EVENING
Jack looks through his mess of a wardrobe for something to wear.
EXT. DISCOUNT CLOTHES STORE - EVENING
Jack looks at clothes displayed outside the store. Boxes of shoes, socks, underwear, undershirts; racks of pants, shirts, etc.
Dress for success.
He walks down the block to another, more upgrade men's clothing store. He looks at men's clothes in the window before entering the store.
INT. CONNIE'S STUDIO APT - EVENING

She sits on the bed. The clothes from cleaners, in a plastic bag, across her lap. She gets up, takes a dress from the wrapped clothes and puts it on. She looks at herself in a tall, skinny, cheap mirror that leans against the wall. 9.99 red sticker still on the frame.
INT. SMALL DINING AREA, CLYDE AND LUCY'S APT - NIGHT
Jack and Connie at the table. Remnants of Chinese food. An uncomfortable silence. Clyde enters from kitchen.
I'm sorry, y'know... to see your Dad like that...
Yeah... In a coma, man.
Clyde takes a couple plates into the kitchen.
... after not seeing him for a while... then...
The coma nurse said when he gasped for air his body did it in a reactive mode. Clyde returns relighting a joint.
Aw, that's... (to Clyde) ...right? Yeah...
Like a dry pump. That's how he put it. I was glad when he left, and I was alone with my dad.
...ahem...hem...that's good, that he left... because... Yeah.
I was glad he left. Yeah.
He was coming on to me.
The coma nurse?
Right there? Yeah.
Right there in the coma room?
That's not right.
He let his hand stay on my arm without saying anything until my dad gasped. Then he left the coma room. My dad was right there. In a coma, but still... That's not right.
No, in the coma room with your dad right there...? In a coma, yeah, but still...
Clyde passes the joint to Connie.
Jack takes it.
INT. DELI #1 - NIGHT
Lucy is digging through the ice cream freezer.
No Chunky Monkey?
No. No Chunky Monkey.
She exits deli.
INT. SMALL DINING AREA, CLYDE AND LUCY'S APT - NIGHT
Clyde offers joint to Connie. She hesitates, takes the joint from Jack, does a small toke. Gives it to Clyde. After a
Just getting your head around that... Yeah...

My dad would gasp and twist his body, y'know, I thought, he wanted to get out of there. So, when I was alone with my dad, I told him it's OK to go, that he didn't have to stick around. I told him I loved him, and that he was a great dad, that he was free to go to heaven. Yeah...
Two days later, he woke up out of the coma. Fuck.
That's... wow... woke up?
... after three months. Fuck.
Clyde passes the joint. Connie takes it, starts to pass it on, does another small hit, passes it to Jack.
INT. DELI #2 - NIGHT
Lucy digs through the freezer of another deli, finds and lifts a carton of Chunky Monkey up. Yes!
Goes to counter.
INT. SMALL DINING AREA, CLYDE AND LUCY'S APT - NIGHT
Connie shakes "no" to the tiny end of a joint offered by Jack, who puts it down in the ashtray.

He talked in a soft voice... like... y'know... I thought he's in like a zombie state. Not like Dawn of the Dead, but a zombie state where you stay around because there's some unfinished business you're responsible for-- like taking care of my mom.
Yeah, your mom, of course...
He went back to be with her at
Sunshine Valley Care Facility.
God, that's... got up from a coma and went home.
They were married fifty-two years.
Fifty-two? Unbelievable... I mean, I believe it, but, wow, fifty-two years.
...yeah, to make sure she was... Then he fell down in the hallway
and hit his head, and then he died.
God...oh... after waking up... a kind of...
Yeah... a miracle coming from a coma, and then...
...cuz he wanted to take care of your mom.
She was blind. She couldn't walk, really. She saw things in the air
near the end. She was blind but she'd look in the air like she was seeing them. I don't know what, y'know... I asked if she was afraid
Connie heads to the bathroom. Jack motions for Clyde to follow him to the front room.
INT. BATHROOM - NIGHT
Connie breathes as though to stem an oncoming panic attack.
INT. FRONT ROOM - NIGHT
Jack and Clyde talk so Connie can't hear them.
I thought, ask about her parents,
She needed to talk about it, I guess. Yeah...
Lucy's gone awhile, right? They probably didn't have Chunky Monkey so she's on a search. Buzzer.
Huh? Huh? How many times you think, "Hey, y'know, they been gone a long time," then, buzz, y'know, they're there.
It's hard to explain.
I don't think you can. Good point. Buzzer. (into intercom) Yo! Who is it?
EXT. APT BUILDING - NIGHT
Lucy's at the intercom.
"Me" I never forget my keys me?
No. Buzz me fucking in me.
Door buzzes and she pushes in.
INT. FRONT ROOM- NIGHT
Clyde's at the opened apt door. Nothing.
It's a game.
INT. BATHROOM - NIGHT
Connie stands in the bathroom at the mirror, distraught, near tears.
INT. FRONT ROOM- NIGHT
Lucy enters the apt and holds up deli bag.
Are you ready for Chunky Monkey?! Sheds her coat and scarf.
Three delis!
-- as she heads into the hall to the kitchen LUCY Where's Connie?
INT. HALL - NIGHT
As Lucy passes the bathroom door, Connie answers from within. I'm here.
CONNIE (O.S.)
Time for Chunky Monkey!
Lucy keeps moving on into the kitchen.
INT. BATHROOM - NIGHT
Connie takes a deep breath. Exits the bathroom.
EXT. STREET - NIGHT
Snow falls. Connie and Jack look for a cab. It'll have to be warmer though.
Yeah. Boating weather. Yeah.
CONNIE
Jack raises his arm to hail an oncoming cab. It passes by without seeing him. They continue on towards the corner intersection.
You sure you don't want me to drive you?
No, really, it's... It's alright. This is plenty. I liked tonight.
They walk a few moments in silence. Winter.
Yeah.

Boating. They continue in silence. Jack observes Connie with the snow falling on her. She looks at him, and he turns away shyly, searches the street for a cab. He sees a cab and steps out to stop it. The cab pulls over and he opens the door. She gets part way in and then impulsively plants an awkward kiss on his cheek and practically stumbles back into the cab, which takes off. Jack stands in the aftermath of the kiss, a kind of slam-kiss.
INT. CLYDE AND LUCY'S APT - NIGHT
Clyde tastes coffee. Hmmm. I'm tempted to say-(interrupts judgment)
Come on, five bucks you can't tell. Buzzer.
(into intercom) JACK (O.S.) (from intercom)
I thought maybe they ran off. (smells coffee, musing) Is it K...?
You're supposed to taste it, and say what it is. Not stall around.
My nose has lost its edge. Your nose goes, your taste goes.
It's not the only thing.
What's that mean?
Jack enters. Snow on him.
It's snowing.
They say, a foot.
Took you some time. No cabs.
Clyde thought maybe you went off together.
I offered to drive but she said it'd be too much trouble. Queens, but I would have.
Coffee's ready.
Maybe a cup before I go. She caught a cab to the "R" train.
You liked Connie?
We got along pretty well outside, ahem... She said she'd like... ahem... She'd like to go boating. Boating?
Rowing in a boat. She said, y'know, to go boating...a-hem, a- hem... sometime, when it's, hem, when...
Jack drinks some water.
So what did you say? What?
When she asked you to go boating.
Maybe, when it's warmer.
You made a date for summer?
No, I'm not, y'know... that bad to say let's do something next summer, y'know, like six months from now.
No, no, no, we know.
It started to snow, and it came up, y'know, warm weather, and it got to (MORE)
summer, to go rowing in a boat. Going boating when it's warmer. said, yeah, but...
If you're afraid, it's not a good idea.
No, I'm not afraid...
No, I know-JACK I don't know if I want to go out in a boat. I can't swim ...
I told you I'd teach you.
There's plenty of time before summer.
Five years ago I said I'd teach you.
He taught me.
You said it caused problems to teach someone you're close to...so, when you asked, later, I thought, you know, I don't think so.
I said it was the swimming lessons.
It wasn't the lessons?
It was humans make mistakes.
It was personal. Yeah.
He's a good teacher.
I should learn, I guess, in case, y'know, I go boating.
While you're learning, do some winter thing, go ice skating... see movies...
By summer, you'll be going away together for weekends to a lake that rents boats. Boating at night. In the moonlight. Huh? Under the stars.
I should learn.
We'll go to the pool uptown. State of the art, ozone filtration.
I'm serious.
Heated?
I'm serious.
It'll get me back going. I used to go three, four times a week.
Well. I better go. (to Lucy) I wanted to play this for you. (to Clyde) You liked it, right? Yeah.
He told me you were into reggae.
It manifests a positive vibe.
Plays portable player. "On the Rivers of Babylon."
Makes me want to manifest ganja.
She exits to the beat of the song.
Here comes the "over I" part. (sings along with song) "...over I"
The song plays on. Lucy returns with a lit joint.
INT. SUBWAY - NIGHT

On the subway back to Queens, Connie looks over Dr. Bob's phone sales information. She studies the Ten Basic Objections to Overcome in Booking Seminars: "Too Busy," "Cash flow," "Talk to Partner," "Took the Last One," etc, committing them to memory. A man is sitting across from her.
I'll bet you're a model. be a model. (pause) A model or actress? You could
You want to know what I do? You know riga mortis? Your face gets distorted. The tongue sticks out
like wood. Your arms and legs get contorted. The hands are in knots.
The man gets up to stand by the doors.
INT. SWIMMING POOL - DAY
Jack and Clyde in the water. A distance away, an ungainly swimmer does a lap to the end, before resting a bit, and heading back.
Let's go a little deeper. Deeper?
You'll still touch. A little
deeper. Come on, a little more. They walk deeper.
It's getting deep.
A little more.
It's pretty deep.
OK. Try it now.
Jack ducks in and out.
That's good, but...Jack. That's good, but go under. Make the bubbles under the water, then come up, take a breath, and go under, bubbles, come up, and get a rhythm
going. Breath, under, bubbles, up.
You'll get used to it. We'll do it together. Ready. But, it's good. Ready. Breath.
They take a breath.
They go under. Make bubbles. Jack resurfaces first.
You can keep your eyes open.
The goggles leak.
They should suck around your eyes a little bit. How do they feel? OK.
They suck around your eyes?
Yeah, I think. Yeah.
See if they leak.
They feel tight.
Go under and see?
Jack dips his head under. Up, quickly.
They seem OK.
You don't have to close your eyes, though.
I want you to visualize.
Visualize?
It's a technique. Close your eyes.
Not open them?
When you do it yeah. But now-- it's something you can do at home, "Visualization," when you're not actually in the water. I want you to close your eyes. OK.
See yourself going under, blowing bubbles, coming up. OK.
Try it. See yourself do it. OK.
He does so. IMAGE: JACK, EYES CLOSED, VISUALIZING HIMSELF. THOUGH IT A42 DOESN'T APPEAR THAT HE IS IN WATER, BUBBLES FLOW FROM HIS NOSE.
Good. Jack. Jack.
Jack stops visualizing.
You visualized? Yeah.
Your eyes were open?
You said close them.
No, to visualize, yeah, when we do it, but, when you visualized you went under, your eyes were open?
I think.
Try it again.
He visualizes going under, eyes open, blowing bubbles. IMAGE: JACK CLOSES HIS EYES AND SEES HIMSELF, EYES WIDE OPEN, HUGE, AND BUBBLES FLOWING FROM HIS NOSE. HE STOPS VISUALIZING.
Yeah. Open.
Good. OK. Look at me when we do it. We'll do it now. Together. Ready. They do it once.
You're doing good.
I can do better. You're doing good.
I can do better.
Jack attempts to do so. The wide-stroking, ungainly swimmer crashes by them.
INT. MTA BUILDING - EVENING
Jack hurries to the door of the employment office of the MTA. It's not open. He's about five minutes late. He taps on the window. A worker inside indicates they are closed.
I just need the job application. The worker shakes his head. Closed. Points to office hours sign on door.
Just the application.
The worker shakes his head. Jack stands a moment in frustration, staring at the worker. Knocks to get the guy's attention, gives him the bird and walks away.
INT. SUBWAY - MORNING

Crowded car. Jack looks across at the MTA EMPLOYMENT poster. His expression turns more critical. He locates the sound of loud chewing and the source of his distraction: a seated subway rider eats potato chips from a bag -- crunching loudly, obliviously-- chips falling on his belly and on to the floor of the car. Jack raises the earphones, presses the button on an older style portable tape player.
INT CLYDE AND LUCY'S APT - MORNING
Lucy in the living room, getting ready for work. She puts the final touches on her makeup, pulls her hair back, talking to Clyde who is in the bathroom.
(to Clyde, off) It's a sales job. That's the bottom line. If you don't close, you don't last.
Clyde, cup of coffee in hand, enters in pajamas, robe, and slippers.
I was talking to you.
I was listening.
What'd I say?
You have to fire Connie.
When do you go in? Tonight. Why?
What's with Jack?
He was upset about a guy at the MTA office. Wouldn't give him an application because he was a minute late.
He needs to hook up. It's time. Shit, I'm running late.
Lucy exits to the outer hallway.
INT. OUTER HALLWAY - (CONTINUOUS)
Clyde follows her to the stairs.
Jack's got me and you, and the limo job. That's it.
That's it for me. I got you. Jack's a friend. I drive a limo.
They head down the stairs.
INT. STAIRS - (CONTINUOUS)
Clyde, coffee in hand, follows her down the stairs.
You take business classes at City. He's talking about the MTA.
I love Jack. But Jack's like, I don't know what to call it. What would you say?
I don't know what it is you don't know what to call.
It's something.
Maybe it's nothing.
She heads down the second landing, Clyde following. She reaches the bottom of the stairs and heads down the hall to the front doors of the apartment building, Clyde after her.
EXT. APT BUILDING - DAY
Clyde follows Lucy from the building to the stoop.
Connie might be good for him. It would be easier to help it along if she were working there, but... and she needs whatever it is... (she turns to him) I'll call.
This is what? This is the Sumatra, right? The Kona!
Lucy continues down the street towards the subway. pulls a joint out of his robe pocket. Lights it.
This is Haze, though. (sings) Purple haze all in my brain Lately things don't seem the same Actin funny-(tokes) Am I happy or in misery? (MORE)
Whatever it is, that girl put a spell on me...
INT CROWDED SUBWAY CAR - MORNING

Connie stands crushed between people in a crowded subway car. She feels a man uncomfortably close, pressing up against her. She squeezes through the crowd. Their eyes meet. She looks away. She looks back to see the man has moved closer in her direction. He's got a bleary smile on his ruddy mug; drunk? a psycho? He keeps moving closer to her.
Keep fucking away from me!
Other riders strain to see what the commotion is at the end of the crowded car.
EXT. BROOKLYN RESIDENTIAL AREA - DAY
Lucy walks down a sidewalk towards 4th Avenue.
EXT. 4TH AVE, BROOKLYN
Lucy turns down 4th Avenue and walks towards the Dr. Bob Thomas Funeral Home.
EXT. DR. BOB THOMAS FUNERAL HOME
Lucy briskly walks up the steps and enters.
INT. DR. BOB THOMAS FUNERAL HOME LOBBY
Lucy crosses the lobby, passing the chapel and the family
counseling rooms. At the end of the interior of the building,
she opens and enters a door to the Grief Seminar offices.
INT. DR. BOB'S GRIEF SEMINAR OFFICES - DAY
Lucy hangs her coat, hat, and scarf and proceeds into the offices towards Connie who is at her workstation. Her back is to Lucy.
If he's there, we can conference. No, I'll hold.
She coughs, winces from a sharp pain in her side.
You're early. I need to talk to you when you're done. Dr. Bob wanted to be here.
Connie winces, holds her side. Lucy sees she's in pain, face bruised, eye with a knot on it.
What happened to you?
I was attacked. No.
On the subway. Yeah. A man. I'll be alright. I'm fine.
Connie coughs up blood.
Get off the phone.
I'm gonna close Curtis-Lopez.
Give me the phone.
Connie moves away, holding her side. Please.
LUCY
I'm going to close!
Lucy digs out her phone to call 911. Connie wipes blood off.
Mr. Curtis? Hi. Mr Lopez. OK. Let's get your credit card reservation done. Get your American Express, sure.
I need an ambulance-A woman was attacked. She's coughing blood. Blood. Coughing. She was attacked on the subway.
INT. HOSPITAL WAITING ROOM - DAY
Clyde enters the waiting area, sees Lucy, who meets him.
She's alright?
Thanks for coming.
Lucky I caught some time. Roads are no picnic.
She takes a call.
Hello. Mr. Kendal, thanks for calling back. Sure. I'll wait, no, I'll wait.
You OK? You sounded pretty worried.
I thought it was internal bleeding but it was blood from her nose that bled backwards.
Backwards? Weird.
Yeah, backwards into your stomach, like a lot. A ruptured dorsal something. She has a couple fractured ribs.
But she's OK, I mean...?
We can say hello soon.
Clyde answers his phone.
Classic Limousine Service. Yeah.
INT. HOSPITAL PARKING STRUCTURE
Jack, phone to ear, parks the limo, gets out. You there?
CLYDE (O.S.)
She's OK. Yeah. Yeah. She OK?
Jack hurries on his way to the hospital.
INT. HOSPITAL WAITING ROOM - DAY
Clyde on phone to Jack. Lucy on hold.
A rupture of the dorsal something
(phone) No, you're worth waiting for.
Clyde shoots a look Lucy's way.
INT. HOSPITAL GIFT SHOP - DAY
Jack enters the hospital. Goes in the gift shop. He's seen through the glass as he looks at the stuffed animals.
INT. HOSPITAL ELEVATOR - DAY
Jack, holding a stuffed animal, rides up in an elevator with a patient on a gurney and a nurse.
INT. HOSPITAL WAITING ROOM - DAY
He gets out of the elevator and walks to the waiting area to join Clyde and Lucy. What do you have?
It's a Koala bear. Hands it to Clyde.
(reads) Authentic Koala Bear. She's OK?
Terrible, huh?
She's OK though?
They said she'll be fine.
He got her a Koala.
Think she'll like it?
Sure, it's cute. I'm gonna see if I can learn anything.
Lucy exits. Jack answers his phone.
Classic Limousine Service. The hospital.
EXT. DINER - DAY
Uncle Frank, on phone to Jack, is exiting the diner with takeout.
So, how's the gal, what's her name?
INT. HOSPITAL WAITING ROOM - DAY
Jack on phone to Uncle Frank.
